title: The Rage of Underpaid Caregivers
dek: You are tired in a way that sleep cannot touch. Your bones ache with the weight of carrying others, and beneath that exhaustion lies a fury so dense it feels like a physical organ. This is not burnout; this is the biological response to a system that demands your lifeblood and offers nothing in return.
bodyMarkdown:
You wake up and the first thing you feel is the ache in your levator scapulae, that thick band of muscle connecting your neck to your shoulder blade. It is tight, ropy, holding the tension of a thousand unsaid words. You are a caregiver. You are underpaid, undervalued, and exhausted in a way that sleep cannot touch. But beneath the exhaustion, there is something else. There is a heat in your belly, a tightness in your jaw. There is rage.

This is not a moral failure. Your anger is not a sign that you are unspiritual or unevolved. It is a biological imperative. The body knows when it is being exploited. The fascia remembers every time you had to smile while swallowing the indignity of a paycheck that barely covers your rent. This is not pathology; this is biology.

## The Anatomy of Exploitation

When you spend your days tending to the needs of others - lifting bodies, wiping faces, soothing fears - your own nervous system is constantly engaged. You are in a state of hyper-vigilance, scanning the environment for danger, anticipating the needs of those who cannot articulate them. This takes a toll. The psoas muscle, deep in your core, tightens. It is the muscle of fight or flight, and it is bracing for an impact that never comes, or perhaps, an impact that is constant and insidious.

Gabor Maté writes extensively about the connection between chronic stress and physical illness. He notes that when we suppress our authentic emotions - like the very justified anger of being underpaid for essential, life-sustaining work - our bodies bear the burden. The immune system compromises. The gut microbiome shifts. The anger does not disappear; it simply turns inward, attacking the self because it is not allowed to attack the system.

## The Myth of the "Calling"

They tell you that caregiving is a calling. They use words like "angel" and "hero" to describe you. But these words are a trap. They are designed to make you feel guilty for wanting fair compensation. If it is a calling, the logic goes, then the reward should be spiritual, not financial. But you cannot pay your electric bill with spiritual rewards. You cannot feed your children with the title of "hero."

This is where the rage becomes a messenger. It is telling you that the exchange is fundamentally unjust. Resmaa Menakem, in his work on somatic abolitionism, teaches us that trauma and systemic oppression are stored in the body. The economic violence inflicted upon caregivers - predominantly women, predominantly women of color - is a form of trauma. Your rage is the body's natural, healthy response to this violence. It is the body saying, "I matter. My labor matters. I deserve to be sustained."

## The Somatics of Fury

Where do you feel it? Is it in the masseter muscle of your jaw, clenched so tight at 3:00 AM that your teeth ache? Is it a burning sensation in your diaphragm, a restriction that makes it hard to take a full, deep breath? Notice it. Do not push it away.

We are taught that anger is ugly, especially in women. We are taught to soften our voices, to be accommodating, to be "nice." But nice does not protect you from exploitation. Nice does not demand a living wage.

Consider the herbs that support this fiery energy. We do not want to extinguish the fire; we want to harness it. [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soothe the frayed edges of a nervous system that has been running on adrenaline for too long. It does not numb the anger; it simply gives the body the resilience to hold it without burning out.

## A Practice for the Psoas

When the rage feels too big, when it threatens to consume you, try this.

Lie on your back on the floor. Place your feet flat, knees bent. Let your arms rest at your sides. Notice the points of contact between your body and the floor. Now, very slowly, let your knees fall open to the sides, bringing the soles of your feet together. Do not force it. Let gravity do the work.

This posture gently opens the pelvic bowl and stretches the psoas. As you lie here, breathe into your lower belly. Imagine the breath reaching all the way down to your pelvic floor. With each exhale, imagine releasing a fraction of the tension held in that deep core muscle. You are not releasing the anger; you are releasing the physical bracing against it. You are making room for the anger to exist without destroying you.

## The System is Broken, Not You

It is crucial to understand that your exhaustion and your rage are not personal failings. You are operating within a system that relies on your unpaid or underpaid labor to function. Capitalism extracts value from caregiving without compensating it fairly because caregiving has historically been considered "women's work," and therefore, free.

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that the body keeps the score. Every missed meal, every double shift, every time you had to choose between buying groceries and paying for a prescription - your body has kept a meticulous ledger. The rage is the body presenting the bill.

## Harnessing the Fire

So what do we do with this rage? We do not swallow it. We do not let it turn into bitterness or despair. We use it.

Anger is the energy of boundaries. It is the energy of "no." It is the energy that says, "This stops here."

You can use this energy to organize, to demand better pay, to unionize. You can use it to set boundaries in your personal life, to say no to extra shifts when your body is screaming for rest. You can use it to advocate for yourself and for your fellow caregivers.

And you must support your physical vessel as you do this work. A [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can provide the deep pressure stimulation your nervous system craves after a day of being pulled in a hundred different directions. It mimics the feeling of being held, which is something caregivers rarely experience.

## The Medicine of Rest

Rest is not a luxury; it is a necessity. It is a radical act of defiance against a system that wants to grind you into dust.

But rest is not just about sleeping. It is about down-regulating the nervous system. It is about finding moments of stillness in the chaos. It is about allowing yourself to be supported, even if it is just by the floor beneath you or the [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) that helps release the stubborn knots in your back.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel guilty when I am angry at the people I care for. What should I do?**
**A:** It is completely normal to feel a mix of love and rage. Your anger is likely not directed at the vulnerable person you are caring for, but at the situation - the lack of support, the inadequate pay, the sheer exhaustion. Acknowledge the anger without judgment. It does not make you a bad caregiver; it makes you a human being operating under impossible conditions.

**Q: How can I advocate for myself when I am already so exhausted?**
**A:** Start small. Advocacy does not always mean leading a protest. It can mean saying no to an unreasonable request. It can mean taking your full lunch break. It can mean talking to your coworkers about your shared struggles. Find allies. You do not have to do this alone.

**Q: Are there specific herbs that can help with the physical symptoms of rage, like a tight jaw or headaches?**
**A:** Yes. Nervines like skullcap and passionflower can be incredibly supportive. They help to relax the musculoskeletal system and calm the mind. Magnesium is also essential; many of us are deficient, and it plays a crucial role in muscle relaxation. But remember, herbs are allies, not cures for systemic injustice. They support your body so you can continue the work of demanding change.

**Q: I feel like my anger is going to destroy me. It's too much.**
**A:** When the anger feels overwhelming, focus on grounding. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice the temperature of the air on your skin. Do the psoas practice described above. If the internal sensation is too intense, focus on the external environment. And please, seek support from a somatic therapist or a trusted community if you can. You do not have to hold this alone.


## The Deepening of the Practice

 The body remembers the injustice. The fascia holds the tension of the unpaid hours. The jaw clenches against the unspoken demands. The breath shortens in the face of systemic neglect. We must breathe into the belly. We must feel the ground beneath our feet. The anger is a fire that can warm us or burn us. We choose to let it warm us. We choose to let it fuel our boundaries. We choose to let it demand justice. The muscles ache, but they are strong. The heart is tired, but it beats with a fierce rhythm. This is the rhythm of survival. This is the rhythm of change. The herbs support us. The earth holds us. We are not alone in this fury. We are part of a lineage of women who have labored and loved and raged. We honor that lineage by honoring our own bodies. We honor our bodies by listening to the rage. The rage is the messenger. The rage is the medicine. The rage is the path forward.  The body remembers the injustice. Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.232Z">May 28, 2026</time>.
In our work on the rage of underpaid caregivers, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
